title_en: Dubai Finals in the Morning, Paris Sake Event in the Afternoon -- The Day FOMUS's "Next Door" Opened
excerpt_en: May 26, 2025 -- In Paris. Morning. In my hotel room, I was getting ready for the online final interview for the Dubai startup selection. I had submitted my materials, passed the preliminary rounds, and practiced extensively for the 8-minute pitch in English.
body_en:
May 26, 2025 -- In Paris

Morning.

In my hotel room, I was getting ready for the online final interview for the Dubai startup selection.

I had submitted my materials, passed the preliminary rounds,

and practiced extensively for the 8-minute pitch in English.

The Morning Challenge: Dubai Final Selection Presentation

The moment Zoom connected, the presentation started right away.

I displayed my script on my phone and timed myself with a stopwatch, completing the presentation in exactly 8 minutes.

The real challenge was the Q&A session that followed.

Since there's no way to practice or predict what they'd ask, I had prepared some anticipated questions and answers,

but the actual questions came from a completely different direction. Haha.

Zoom's auto-translation wasn't available in this setup either,

so I had them type their questions in the chat,

and somehow managed to respond.

Honestly, my business English still needs a lot of work,

but I think it was really meaningful that I, as the company's representative, stepped up and took on the challenge myself.

And then, the result --

Amazingly, I received notice that I'd passed the selection just a few hours later.

They had said it would take up to a week to notify us,

but it was so fast that I actually wondered, "Is this a scam?" Haha.

In any case, establishing a FOMUS entity in Dubai and moving forward became a real option.

The official decision will come

after I finish all my scheduled commitments here in Paris.

A new future is about to unfold.

Kura Master Main Event: The Afternoon Scene Buzzing with Paris Energy

In the afternoon, it was the main day of "Kura Master," one of Paris's largest sake events, which I've been participating in.

Yesterday I was a photographer, and today I joined as a sake service staff member.

During my free time, I walked around the exhibition booths,

making many new connections and gaining fresh inspiration.

Sake x Paris x FOMUS

Over 400 people attended this event.

What surprised me was how deeply interested and passionate the French and other European attendees were about sake.

In fact, many of them were more knowledgeable and enthusiastic about tasting than Japanese people,

and honestly, that scene moved me.

But it also raised a question in my mind.

"Is it really ideal to offer FOMUS masu as tasting cups?"

The thing is, sake enthusiasts want to carefully savor the aroma and flavor using glasses,

and the hinoki (cypress) scent that comes with masu

could potentially interfere with that experience.

That's when I realized again that

rather than positioning masu as "sake cups," it might be better suited to present them as "gifts with a cultural story."

FOMUS is a communication tool and a vessel for self-expression.

Presenting it as a souvenir, gift, or good-luck charm might be more fitting for the French market.

Insights and Confidence from the After-Party

After the event, the organizers and volunteers headed to a Vietnamese restaurant for a celebratory dinner.

I sat next to the person who had invited me to this event,

and I prioritized listening to the organizing team over chatting with people my own age.

Because what matters most for FOMUS is,

  • *not "generating immediate sales," but

"building awareness and credibility for the FOMUS brand."**

The track record of "FOMUS masu being featured at Kura Master"

will serve as a "business card" for expanding into other countries going forward.

So I've decided

to dedicate all my time in France to this "seed-planting" work.

The Resolve to Leave a Lasting Impression

Honestly, I couldn't talk to everyone.

But I think I managed to connect with as many people as I could, leaving a strong impression of FOMUS and my passion.

It's okay even if I fail.

The worst thing is leaving no impression at all.

With that mindset driving me,

I was actually invited to another after-party tomorrow.

I'm going to make the most of this opportunity.

excerpt_en: 2025/05/26. So, first up, in the morning I had the final selection for the Dubai startup program online. This was a hugely important matter, and I practiced my English presentation during every spare moment. Working on pronunciation to communicate clearly,
body_en:
2025/05/26

So,

first up, in the morning I had the final selection for the Dubai startup program online.

This was a hugely important matter,

and I practiced my English presentation during every spare moment.

Working on pronunciation to communicate clearly,

figuring out how to fit everything

within the 8-minute time limit, and so on.

And then, the real deal.

Once the Zoom call for the online presentation connected,

I jumped right into my pitch.

Since I'd practiced so many times,

I was able to deliver the presentation in exactly 8 minutes.

For the online session, I had my script ready on my phone,

and I was measuring the 8 minutes with a stopwatch,

so timing wasn't an issue at all.

The scariest part was the feedback & question time.

Since I had no idea what they'd ask,

I had compiled a list of likely questions,

but unfortunately, none of those questions came up.

My business English is still pretty rough,

and since it was on Zoom, there was no auto-translation available,

so I ended up having them type their questions in the chat,

and somehow managed to communicate what I wanted to say.

If I'd had someone to translate for me,

that problem could have been avoided,

but unfortunately, I was on my own this time.

That said, as the company's representative,

putting myself directly on the front line

is extremely important,

so in the end, I think it worked out well.

After finishing the presentation and Q&A,

that was it.

Now all I had to do was wait for the results.

I had breakfast at the hotel,

and started preparing for the Paris

sake event "Kura Master" starting in the afternoon.

Meanwhile, I got a message from Dubai,

and guess what -- I passed!

They said results would come within a week,

but it was way faster than expected,

and I'm honestly questioning whether it's legit. Haha.

So,

as one new option, establishing a corporate entity in Dubai

and moving forward as a startup

has been added to the mix.

I'll make the final decision after I've wrapped up all my plans in Paris.

Kura Master Main Event

So, in the afternoon, I headed to the Paris sake event "Kura Master."

Following yesterday, I participated as a volunteer staff member.

Today I wasn't a photographer,

but a sake service staff member.

However, I had a bit of free time,

so I checked out the exhibition booths, chatted with people,

and got some great opportunities for new connections.

In Paris,

there are more people interested in sake than in other countries,

and apparently over 400 people attended this event.

While I was serving sake,

seeing French people and visitors from other European countries

showing so much interest in sake

and tasting it so enthusiastically

was truly surprising,

and I was moved that there are people who love sake even more than Japanese people do.

And that's when I started thinking,

is it really the right move to offer masu

as a tool for drinking sake here??

People who love sake enjoy savoring the aroma and taste from a glass, so the distinctive scent of masu shouldn't get in the way of that.

So rather than using them for tasting,

positioning masu as one of the items

to enjoy alongside the sake experience,

or emphasizing their story and cultural significance

and focusing on gifts and souvenirs,

seemed like a better approach.

These are insights I gained from actually

seeing the customers in person.

Rather than introducing masu as sake cups

in a place where masu culture doesn't exist,

maybe it's better to develop them as the communication tools

and self-expression items that FOMUS aims for,

as well as souvenirs and gifts,

and use the fact that FOMUS masu are spreading through French events as a selling point to expand into other regions?

That's what I'm thinking.

That said, there's still a lot more research needed,

so I plan to keep showing up at events like these.

The after-party matters too

After cleaning up from the event,

the organizers and volunteers went out for a celebration.

We had Vietnamese food.

I sat near the person who invited me to the event, at a different table from the volunteer staff.

It would have been fun to hang out and have a good time with people close to my age,

but my purpose is ultimately to expand the FOMUS brand globally, so I sat near the Kura Master organizers

and got to hear all kinds of things you wouldn't normally get to hear.

I know running events is really tough,

so hearing the specific details was very informative,

and I thought it would be essential knowledge

if FOMUS ever puts on a big event in the future.

This time, my top priority was getting the Kura Master organizers to know about FOMUS and about me,

not selling masu right away.

In the long term, if there's something we can do together,

I'd love to make it happen.

More than immediate sales,

the credibility of "FOMUS masu being featured at a Paris sake event"

is what matters most.

For that,

I'll dedicate all my time in France to this cause.

I believe this is the ultimate grassroots hustle.

My stay is limited,

so I'm going to leave as big an impression as I possibly can.

I don't mind if my bold approach rubs someone the wrong way.

It's sadder to not be recognized at all. Haha.

So,

I think I managed to make my presence felt pretty well.

And guess what,

I was invited to the after-party again tomorrow!

I'm going to seize this chance!!

excerpt_en: May 27. So, barely any time had passed since the Kura Master event ended. Starting at noon, I had a lunch meeting. A meeting with the person who invited me to this event. Enjoying some fancy French cuisine while discussing business.
body_en:
May 27

So, barely any time had passed

since the Kura Master event ended.

Starting at noon, I had a lunch meeting.

A meeting with the person

who invited me to this event.

Enjoying some fancy French cuisine

while discussing all sorts of business topics.

I personally want to expand the FOMUS brand even further in France, so I'd like to collaborate in any way I can.

The professional conduct

I learned between ages 22 and 24,

the French cuisine and wine skills

I picked up during my days as a hotel concierge,

and the expertise in

masu, photography, AI, and web3 that I gained between ages 25 and 29 --

it's precisely because I've built

knowledge and experience

across these different fields

that I can differentiate myself from others.

This stay in France has really reminded me

that none of my past experiences were wasted.

Besides the person who invited me and myself,

there was also someone at the table

who's expanding an original glassware brand worldwide,

and talking with someone who's actually taking their brand global

was incredibly fascinating.

It made me realize I need to be more active and aggressively promote my products.

It was a real wake-up call.

After the lunch meeting,

I headed to a cafe.

A cafe run by a Japanese owner who had also exhibited at Kura Master.

Here, I had yet another meeting.

The fact that a Japanese person owns and runs

a shop right in the heart of Paris

is genuinely impressive,

and I felt like I'd love to be involved in some way.

They serve a delicious hazelnut dessert

paired with champagne,

and to increase the average spend per customer,

they deliberately pair the dessert with champagne

instead of coffee.

Little things like that are really clever.

The rent for this cafe is apparently 500,000 yen a month,

which is not cheap at all.

It really drove home how tough it is

to have your own physical shop.

Today was packed with dinner meetings

After the cafe meeting ended and we parted ways,

next up was a dinner with the Kura Master organizing team.

Since I was already stuffed from the lunch and afternoon meetings,

I decided to walk about an hour

to the next venue.

While walking, I listened to business podcasts

and checked on music I've been composing.

There's always something to do.

I arrived a bit early,

so I did some work at a nearby park,

then headed to the Kura Master office.

There,

I did a Japanese wine tasting with the organizers.

There must have been about 7 bottles in total.

When you're traveling around the world,

even when you find Japanese sake or wine,

the prices are so high that you can rarely afford to drink them.

It's because of connections like these that come from being in France

that I've been able to taste so many,

and I'm nothing but grateful.

Back in my hotel concierge days,

I used to practice by drinking wine endlessly,

so it had been a while since I'd been to a tasting like this,

and I really enjoyed it.

After that, we went to dinner.

The Kura Master organizers and their representative, Kei-san.

And from the volunteers who came from outside France,

it was me plus two others who were invited.

There,

I got to hear all sorts of stories again.

Out of all the volunteer staff,

why were only the three of us invited?

Well, here's the thing --

the volunteers who live in France

were apparently given Amazon gift cards,

but since we don't live in France,

we can't use them. Haha.

So instead,

they treated us to a special dinner.

From my perspective,

getting to sit down and talk with the organizers like this was the perfect opportunity to get them to know "MaSU,"

and I'm glad they didn't just hand me an Amazon gift card and call it a day. Haha.

It's precisely because I volunteered for free these past two days

that I was given an opportunity like this,

and it reminded me once again

how important it is to give first.

Nothing is confirmed yet,

but there was even talk of creating a Kura Master trophy using FOMUS masu,

and if I can make something really high-end and impressive,

they might actually adopt it.

I never expected to be presented with an opportunity like this,

so I'm going to go after this one seriously.

excerpt_en: May 28, 2025. The whirlwind sake event "Kura Master" has come to a successful close, and my scheduled commitments in Paris are now done for the time being. I thought I could finally relax a bit... but there's absolutely no room for that.
body_en:
May 28, 2025

The whirlwind sake event "Kura Master" has

come to a successful close,

and my scheduled commitments in Paris are now done for the time being.

I thought I could finally relax a bit... but

there's absolutely no room for that. Haha.

A Long-Awaited "Day Off"... More Like a "Mountain of Tasks" Day

Today was a day to crush all the tasks that had been piling up over the past few days.

  • Editing photos from the model shoot in Lithuania
  • Translating and organizing the massive amount of English documents related to the Dubai startup
  • Editing photos I took as a photographer on the first day of Kura Master

Plus a ton of other small tasks.

The word "rest" is nowhere to be found. Haha.

There were places I wanted to visit, but...

During the event, I was introduced to

some wonderful spots.

I really wanted to go check them out,

but with this workload, it just wasn't possible.

What I need right now is to move forward, not sightsee.

So,

I holed up and focused on work all day.

Where's the French cuisine? Tonight's dinner is "that old standby"

For dinner, I had frozen paella from the nearby supermarket "Lidl."

About 700 yen. Cheap, and all you do is microwave it.

This is just right for me.

Actually, this was something I used to eat all the time back when I was in Ireland.

"Cheap, fast, and decent" --

the perfect partner for someone swamped with work like me right now.

What happened to last night's French full course...?

There's not even a hint of "French" in tonight's dinner. Haha.

The Reality of One of Paris's Cheapest Hotels

My accommodation this time is one of the more budget-friendly hotels in Paris.

As a result,

cockroaches make fairly frequent appearances in the kitchen.

It's hardly an environment where you'd want to cook,

so frozen paella that only needs a microwave is truly a blessing.

Balancing comfortable living conditions with the ability to stay active is tough,

but right now I'm prioritizing "being able to move."

Even After Kura Master Ends, the Work Doesn't

Even though the event has wrapped up,

my own work is far from over.

In fact, this is where the real work begins.

The shoots in Lithuania, the new developments in Dubai, and the future strategy for FOMUS.

I'm going to keep on running.

Going from French cuisine to frozen paella on the road --

I actually kind of like this feeling.

This was a real, behind-the-scenes day away from the glamorous spotlight.

title_en: Facing the Mountain of Work II
excerpt_en: May 28, 2025 -- In Paris. The whirlwind sake event "Kura Master" has come to a successful close, and my scheduled commitments in Paris are now done for the time being. I thought I could finally relax a bit... but there's absolutely no room for that.

and my scheduled commitments in Paris are now done for the time being.

I thought I could finally relax a bit... but

there's absolutely no room for that. Haha.

A Long-Awaited "Day Off"... More Like a "Mountain of Tasks" Day

title_en: The Challenge in Ireland: Making the Next Move
excerpt_en: May 29. Today was a day focused on administrative work. I wanted the chance to explore traditional French craftsmanship, but right now I'm dedicating myself to tackling the tasks in front of me one by one. Since my work is different every day,
body_en:
May 29

Today was a day focused on administrative work.

I wanted the chance to explore traditional French craftsmanship, but right now I'm dedicating myself to tackling the tasks in front of me one by one.

Since my work is different every day,

I constantly need to adapt to new situations, but this has been a great learning opportunity for me.

Then, I decided to leave France on short notice.

Since it was a last-minute flight, it was more expensive than usual, but I decided it would be more cost-effective and beneficial for me to head to the next country rather than continue staying in France.

I went ahead and booked a flight and accommodation, and decided to head to London, which I hadn't visited yet.

I'd always thought I could go anytime while I was in Ireland, but the opportunity never came, so the timing ended up being now.

In London, I plan to enjoy some sightseeing while also scouting for places where I can expand "FOMUS."

Schedule from June Onward:

Strategy Outside the Schengen Zone

I need to finalize my schedule from June onward as soon as possible. My Ireland working holiday visa expires on July 28, and hotel prices in Europe skyrocket during this period.

I also need to consider the Schengen Agreement's "90 days within 180 days" rule. Since I've already been in the Schengen zone for close to 80 days, I'd prefer to stay outside the Schengen area until mid-July when the 90-day rule resets, to avoid unintentionally violating the regulations.

So, I had previously reached out about a live-in volunteer position at a castle in Tullamore, about an hour from Dublin, where I had participated in a cleanup volunteer activity in early May.

They've been considering me as a candidate, and we're currently in negotiations.

If I get permission, I plan to stay as a live-in volunteer at the castle from mid-June to mid-July. During this period, I want to focus on activities like:

  • Continuing Masu Photo shoots in Ireland
  • Strengthening my AI skills
  • Building the foundation for podcast/radio distribution
  • Writing the novel for FOMUS's original character "KUKU"

Ideally, I'd dedicate June and July to the live-in volunteer work at the castle, and spend all my free time on creative projects.

Then, starting around August, I plan to establish a corporate entity in Dubai and begin expanding FOMUS globally in earnest.

Commitment to Ireland:

A One-of-a-Kind Approach

Since mid-June last year, I've been traveling the world to expand FOMUS, and living temporarily in Ireland allowed me to build connections with the Japanese Embassy.

However, my goal of collaborating with Irish whiskey and Guinness brands hasn't progressed much.

This is because most Japanese business owners in Ireland run their businesses without getting deeply involved in the local whiskey or Guinness industries.

In other words, what I need to do is break into the Irish business community on my own.

To do that, it's important to communicate that I'm deeply committed to Ireland.

The fact that I'm working as an international volunteer at an Irish castle would be a powerful asset.

I'm the only one who can take this kind of unique approach, thanks to my extraordinary mobility and the ability to dedicate my time to being on the ground.

And as the company's representative,

I'm prepared to invest more time than anyone else.

What I learned when I first came to Ireland was that even after living here for just one year, there are no Japanese business owners who have connections to the Irish business community -- specifically in the whiskey and Guinness industries that I want to connect with.

From that point on, I vaguely felt that in order for "MaSU" and "FOMUS" to partner with Irish Guinness and whiskey, I would need an approach different from typical business owners.

I honestly don't know if volunteering at a castle will directly lead to collaborations with Guinness or whiskey brands.

However, given that so many people supported us through the 2024 crowdfunding campaign, it would be frustrating to leave Ireland without producing any results.

Producing results within a year would have been ideal, but I've also come to painfully realize that I'm not quite there yet in terms of capability.

The Final 2 Months: The Grand Finale in Ireland

So, what can I do right now?

In 2025, I've been focusing my activities on countries other than Ireland. As a result, I became interested in the Dubai startup program and was invited to Kura Master in Paris.

As my working holiday period nears its end, I want to recommit to Ireland in June and July, and even if it doesn't happen this year, I strongly hope to make the FOMUS-Ireland collaboration a reality.

And I want to show everyone the moment when "MaSU actually collaborates with Irish whiskey and Guinness to expand the brand worldwide!"

I've gone off on a bit of a tangent,

but honestly, hardly anything is going well for me right now.

Still, connections have been forming wherever I go, and that's what brought me to where I am today. But the collaboration with Ireland that I originally set my sights on is still limited to the connection with the embassy.

People tell me that's already a big achievement, but I want to collaborate with Irish companies in some meaningful way.

So, I'm focusing my efforts on Ireland for the remaining 2 months and actively negotiating to participate in the live-in volunteer program at the castle.

If I'm going to stay in Ireland, I'd rather do it boldly as a live-in castle volunteer than just quietly paying rent and doing small-scale activities.

There probably aren't many Japanese people who can pull something like this off. Haha.

After all, it's volunteer work.

Working holiday people earning a living through part-time jobs,

or people working at Japanese IT companies can't do this.

They all have homes to go to, after all. Haha.

In my case, I can work online, and I currently don't have a home!

I'm going to use every bit of my skills, circumstances, and connections

to form a hypothesis and take action to produce results!

excerpt_en: May 31. London activities are a go! So, I had the whole day free today, and it was time for field research on art and traditional crafts. First, I headed to a Japanese concept store. It was about a 20-minute walk from the hotel, so I walked there.
body_en:
May 31

London activities are a go!

So,

I had the whole day free today,

and it was time for field research on art and traditional crafts.

First, I headed to a Japanese concept store.

It was about a 20-minute walk from the hotel,

so I walked there.

They were currently hosting an event about wood and traditional crafts,

which was very relevant to FOMUS,

so I made sure to check it out properly.

The exhibition showcased artisan techniques through documents and actual demonstrations of wood joinery and other methods.

Personally, I really liked the kumiko (traditional wooden lattice) folding screen.

There was also a section selling Japanese items,

and they had masu there too.

Placed quietly in the sake corner,

they were probably made by the same manufacturer as FOMUS masu, and a one-go masu was being sold for 30 pounds.

That's about 5,800 yen in Japanese currency.

Retail prices vary quite a bit depending on the country,

and the fact that things still aren't standardized

has become clear from traveling the world.

In other words,

this is a market that still has plenty of opportunity.

I casually asked the staff how one would go about placing products here,

and apparently there's a company in London that supplies crafts,

and they stock whatever that company discovers and brings in.

I saw several items that I had purchased in Japan,

and they were being sold at 3 to 5 times the price.

Even accounting for customs duties,

the math works out to a solid profit.

If I could get FOMUS's jewelry brand "FOMUS PARURE" placed here, I think there would be demand since the designs are really cute.

Obviously, showing up out of the blue without any materials

and saying "I'd like you to carry masu accessories"

would be way too reckless,

so I didn't push the conversation that far,

but I definitely see potential.

The Design Museum

Next up,

London's Design Museum.

It's one of the world's leading museums dedicated to contemporary design, covering a wide range of fields including product, graphic, fashion, and architectural design.

Founded in 1989 by Sir Terence Conran, it relocated in 2016 to a 1960s landmark building (the former Commonwealth Institute) on Kensington High Street.

The building was transformed into a contemporary museum by John Pawson's design, and it won the European Museum of the Year Award in 2018.

What I personally found most interesting was

the permanent exhibition "Designer Maker User,"

which explores the design process from the perspectives of the designer, the maker, and the user.

FOMUS products also have

different designers, makers, and users,

so I realized there are so many more perspectives from which to examine them.

Victoria and Albert Museum (V&A)

Next, the Victoria and Albert Museum (V&A).

The V&A, located in South Kensington, London, is one of the world's largest museums of decorative arts and design.

Founded in 1852, it was named after Queen Victoria and Prince Albert.

It houses approximately 2.8 million objects, spanning a wide range of fields including art, design, crafts, fashion, photography, and architecture.

There was a huge collection of jewelry on display,

and I found things that could potentially be used for FOMUS PARURE,

as well as ideas for accessories that would look interesting on the characters from our character project "KUKU."

Natural History Museum

And then,

I also visited the Natural History Museum.

Located in South Kensington, London, it's one of the world's premier natural science museums.

Originally part of the British Museum's natural history department, it became independent in 1881, and currently houses approximately 80 million specimens.

With its magnificent architecture and diverse exhibits, it attracts millions of visitors annually.

Since it was Saturday,

it was absolutely packed.

This museum has dinosaurs and such,

so unlike other museums, there were tons of kids.

It's a great place for families.

I've visited natural history museums in various countries,

so I just did a quick walkthrough.

Along the way, I spotted a machine where you could buy pressed medals,

which was the same as what I saw at Palm Jumeirah in Dubai.

FOMUS is planning to launch a capsule toy business

sometime this year or next,

so I took note for reference.

During lunch,

I gave in to temptation

and ended up eating at Marugame Seimen, paying about 1,500 yen.

The taste inevitably falls short of what you'd get in Japan,

but it was definitely udon.

A bit pricey for a solo meal,

but I got a sense of the level of udon that overseas customers are eating.

So yeah, that's how it went --

walking around London while constantly thinking about

whether there's any opportunity for FOMUS to break in.

Especially since 2025 started,

I've been paying close attention to souvenir products.

That's because FOMUS has plans to launch a project targeting the gift and souvenir market.

While I can still move freely, I want to see as much as possible,

so that I can quickly scale the business from there.

I'll keep exploring the world while I still can.

excerpt_en: June 1: It's now June 2025, and I'm still active overseas. Currently staying in London. After finishing my online work, I've been finding time to visit London's major museums and galleries. The main purpose is to search for new ideas for the masu brand
body_en:
June 1:

It's now June 2025, and I'm still active overseas. Currently staying in London.

After finishing my online work, I've been finding time to visit London's major museums and galleries.

The main purpose is to search for new ideas for the masu brand "FOMUS" and the character project "KUKU."

The more knowledge I have in my repertoire, the more I can apply it to various business ventures and come up with new ideas. If I, as the company's representative, lack knowledge, I can't come up with ideas in the first place.

That's why I make it a point to visit as many places as I can, even if it costs money and time.

The British Museum: The Weight of History and Ethical Debates

Today, I visited the British Museum, which I had booked in advance.

Since it was Sunday, the crowds were incredible. So I decided to focus on the main exhibits and the specific sections that caught my interest. Browsing a museum in those crowds is quite the challenge. Haha.

The most popular attraction was, of course, the Rosetta Stone. This stele, which was the key to deciphering ancient Egyptian writing, continues to spark historical interest and a sense of wonder in countless people.

What personally struck me was the real Moai statue.

Unlike the Moai statues standing outdoors on Easter Island, this one is displayed indoors, so its preservation is exceptional. The facial expressions and the carvings on the back (including engravings indicating the Birdman cult) were remarkably clear.

This Moai statue is also one of the objects that the Rapa Nui people of Easter Island have formally requested be returned, making it a symbol of the international cultural property repatriation debate.

The Parthenon Sculptures (Elgin Marbles) were also on display. Greece has been strongly demanding their return for years, making them another symbol of the cultural property repatriation debate.

As many of you may know, a large number of the British Museum's collection was acquired through war, colonial rule, or unequal treaties and transactions -- methods that were "legal but ethically questionable."

[The Legitimacy of Ownership and the Repatriation Issue]

  • Background: The Era of the British Empire

From the 18th century to the early 20th century, Britain built the world's largest colonial empire. In the process, local cultural artifacts, religious relics, and artworks were "collected," and many were transported to the British Museum and other British institutions.

  • The Current Debate

The British Museum maintains that its acquisitions were "legal under international law at the time," but considering the backdrop of colonialism, there is a growing voice that such practices are difficult to justify by modern ethical and human rights standards. In recent years, African nations, Greece, Egypt, and others have formally demanded the return of artifacts, and the global movement toward cultural property repatriation is accelerating.

  • Current Developments

While some items like the Benin Bronzes have begun to be returned, the British Museum Act places legal constraints that in principle prevent the museum from returning items in its collection, making repatriation far from straightforward. Compromise solutions such as "joint custody" and "long-term loans" are also being explored.

  • An Ethical Perspective

While there is a side that has contributed to the preservation of knowledge and culture, the fundamental issue is the asymmetric power dynamics between the countries that took the artifacts and the people of the original cultures, which cannot be ignored.

Despite the various issues it carries, from a visitor's perspective, being able to see such precious artifacts in Britain is a good thing in its own way, I felt.

Japan Centre: Expanding the Possibilities for Masu

Next, I headed to London's "Japan Centre." Established in 1976, it's the UK's largest specialist store for Japanese food and culture, offering a wide selection of Japanese ingredients and products.

They had masu here too, but they were cheap-looking plastic ones. It gave off the impression of "just stick it next to the sake section and call it a day!" -- something I'd definitely like to change in the near future.

Tate Modern: Contemporary Art and a Brief Respite

After that, I went to the Tate Modern. Located in the Bankside area of London, it's one of Britain's premier modern art museums. It opened in 2000 and showcases modern and contemporary art from around the world.

There were works by various famous artists, but during the later part of the visit, there was a video installation you watch while lying down -- and since I'd been constantly on the move doing research and then going back to the hotel to work on FOMUS stuff, I was so exhausted that I completely passed out. Haha. Installations where you lie down to watch, especially planetariums, always make me fall asleep.

One piece that I found particularly interesting was a work that used shadows. That was really fascinating.

London's Prices and Self-Catering Life

London's cost of living is high, and compared to Latvia or Lithuania, everything feels expensive.

So while I eat out for lunch, I cook for myself at night. Pasta is the staple, and I add whatever is on sale at the supermarket each day. Today, vegetables were half-price, so I grabbed some and threw them into my pasta, and it turned out incredibly good.

It's been almost a year since I started traveling the world.

My cooking skills, though, are the one thing that just won't improve. Haha.

title_en: London, the Beginning of a New Adventure
excerpt_en: May 30, 2025: Travel day from Paris to London. After taking a breather at the Charles de Gaulle Airport lounge, it was time to depart. There was only one lounge available with my Priority Pass, and the food was just snacks,
body_en:
May 30, 2025:

Travel day from Paris to London.

After taking a breather at the Charles de Gaulle Airport lounge, it was time to depart.

There was only one lounge available with my Priority Pass, and the food was just snacks, but it was way better than eating at the expensive airport cafes.

The flight from Paris to London was about one hour.

This time I arrived at Southend Airport on the outskirts of London, and from there it took about an hour and a half to reach central London. Since my hotel was on the west side, I had to transfer trains a few more times.

Actually, this is my first time visiting central London. I'd used airports for layovers before, but had never actually gone outside.

The UK is the 35th country I've visited, and navigating European public transportation is second nature to me by now.

Living this nomadic lifestyle, you pick up more and more skills, and even small things like this build your confidence.

My stay in the UK this time is planned for 5 nights.

Since I didn't check in until 6 PM today, I couldn't do much on the first day, but I plan to go out and do field research over the remaining 4 days.

I'm not particularly planning any Masu Photo shoots, but it might be nice to shoot some short-form video content as a street photographer.

The point is, I want to do things I can only do on the ground while brainstorming ideas for expanding FOMUS.

title_en: Officially a Dubai Government-Recognized Startup: Opening a New Door
excerpt_en: 2025/06/02. So, remember that startup presentation I mentioned before? I actually passed! What I got accepted into is a Dubai government-recognized startup support community. Normally, setting up a corporate entity in Dubai comes with various restrictions,
body_en:
2025/06/02

So,

remember that startup presentation I mentioned before?

I actually passed!

What I got accepted into is a Dubai government-recognized startup support community.

Normally, setting up a corporate entity in Dubai comes with various restrictions, and the registration costs are quite high.

However, with government recognition, some registration fees are waived or reduced by half, along with many other benefits. I had been thinking that FOMUS needed a base outside of Japan to expand globally.

Back in March this year, I visited Dubai on a scouting trip with absolutely no connections, and inspired by that experience, I started researching Dubai startups and discovered this government support system.

From there, I'd been steadily working on my application materials while staying in Latvia. I passed the first screening while I was in Lithuania, and then gave my final presentation while I was in Paris.

And the other day, I received word that FOMUS had been approved as a Dubai government-recognized startup, with permission to receive support for establishing a Dubai entity.

Preparing for the Dubai Entity: Challenges Ahead

Today was the meeting for that. I had studied the materials they sent in advance, but there are quite a few differences between Japanese and Dubai corporate entities.

An easy-to-understand example is "licensing."

For a Japanese corporation, you just describe your business activities in the articles of incorporation during registration, and you're free to conduct those activities.

But with a Dubai entity, you can't just do whatever you want after incorporation.

You can only conduct business activities approved by the Dubai government. For instance, if you only have an e-commerce license for selling masu, and you go ahead and host events without authorization, you'd need a separate license -- and without one, you'd face fines.

That's just one example, but there are all sorts of restrictions like this. However, since there's zero tax, if you can navigate it well, it can become an incredibly powerful corporate entity.

So even since arriving in London, I've been studying up on the Dubai entity every day. Squeezing in visits to London's museums in between all of that. (I actually am working, you know!)

Leading up to today's meeting, I was also swamped with preparing the necessary documents -- selecting the right licenses, choosing the company name, determining share distribution, and more. I also had to read through a licensing manual of over 130 pages. The to-do list was endless.

But thanks to having an AI partner now, I've been able to interpret all of this much more efficiently.

Honestly, if I hadn't been making good use of AI, I probably wouldn't have passed this Dubai government-recognized startup program. Haha.

Cutting-edge technology really should be used to the fullest.

In today's meeting, we collaboratively worked on preparations for establishing the government-recognized entity, and the application is currently pending.

Apparently, approval should come within 5 days.

After that, there's the registration fee for establishing the entity, a year's worth of coworking space fees to join the startup community, visa applications, and so on. Most of it is just wire transfers, and once those are complete, the Dubai entity will be established.

The Significance of the Dubai Entity:

A Foothold for Global Expansion

You might be thinking, "What's the point of creating a Dubai entity?" -- but there's a big reason. It means having opportunities to connect with global investors.

Currently, I'm the representative of the Japanese corporation FOMUS, staying long-term in Ireland on a working holiday visa, and traveling around the world building connections as an extension of that.

Honestly, in business-to-business dealings, I'm just "MaSU who has a Japanese company," and to the other party, I'm a foreigner.

However, having a Dubai entity, and moreover being a Dubai government-recognized startup, serves as a significant credential when negotiating with companies around the world.

The Dubai government doesn't just hand out government-recognized status to anyone, so this builds substantial credibility. And it makes it much easier to build connections with investors.

Over the past six months traveling the world, honestly, Dubai was pretty much the only place where I thought, "I'd actually want to live here." Haha.

The summer heat is intense, I'm sure, but as a business hub, it's the best.

I decided that spending another year flying around without a base wouldn't generate significant revenue for the FOMUS brand.

That's why I want to force a change in my environment, dive into a place full of business pros, and see if a whole new world opens up.

While traveling the world all this time,

I hadn't really been able to create touchpoints with investors.

Honestly, I have the drive and the ability to make things happen. But that requires budget.

Currently, FOMUS's revenue isn't that high, so it's about time I need some large B2B orders. And with a Dubai entity, the tax is zero.

The Japanese entity FOMUS and the Dubai entity FOMUS have similar but different business scopes.

I'll go into the details another time, but essentially, the Japanese entity FOMUS is for nurturing the creative side and the brand, while the Dubai entity FOMUS is for earning revenue overseas. If I can make both work in tandem, I'll be able to take on even bigger challenges than before.

There are many other reasons as well, but these are the big ones. FOMUS has multiple projects, and among them:

  • FOMUS PARURE's global expansion
  • Anime adaptation of the character "KUKU"
  • MASUKAME capsule toy business
  • Overseas expansion of Masu Wall

These are businesses with the potential to expand not just in Japan but worldwide.

I want to properly generate profits overseas and reinvest a portion of that back into the Japanese operations.

Masu is still largely unknown, which means there are endless possibilities for how to approach it, including collaborations with sake and cuisine. There's also potential in the souvenir and gift market.

I'm moving forward proactively with establishing the Dubai entity so I can properly capture these opportunities.

Of course, the setup costs aren't cheap.

But if taking this risk opens up new opportunities, then I'm ready to commit. I never imagined I'd be starting a company in Dubai right after turning 30, but maybe that's just the most "MaSU-like" thing to do.

I'm going to keep on challenging myself!

By the way, I also did some field research in London today,

but I'll save that story for tomorrow's post.
